input.background: The Multiple Risk Factor Intervention Trial (MRFIT is pronounced Mister Fit) was conducted in the late 1970s and early 1980s to test the effectiveness of coronary disease prevention programs. Study subjects were randomly assigned to either a treatment group that received special interventions intended to reduce the risk of heart disease or a control group that received their usual sources of health care.
input.question: Why is the use of a concurrent control group important?

output.answer: This shows the importance of using a concurrent control group to establish the effect of a treatment.

treatment_group.value: Subjects who received special interventions intended to reduce the risk of heart disease
treatment_group.description: The group of study subjects that received specific interventions to reduce heart disease risk.

control_group.value: Subjects who received their usual sources of health care
control_group.description: The group of study subjects that did not receive special interventions and continued with their usual health care.

heart_disease_risk.description: The outcome variable measuring the risk of developing coronary disease in study subjects.
